stem:
A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents to the emergency department with 36 hours of left calf pain and swelling that started after a minor abrasion at a fishing trip. The pain is now disproportionate to what is visible. He is febrile and rigoring. On examination the left calf is tense, dusky red with a faint dishwater fluid blister proximally, and exquisitely tender beyond the visible margin. There is no obvious crepitus. He has palpable but reduced foot pulses bilaterally. His vital signs and laboratory results are on the trolley.

The ED senior is debating between cellulitis and a surgical emergency.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 122/min, blood pressure 96/62 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 39.1°C
data_table.Examination: Left calf tense and dusky; pain extends beyond visible erythema; one fluid-filled blister; foot pulses palpable; sensation reduced over the calf
data_table.Investigations: CRP 240 mg/L; WCC 18.5 x10^9/L; Hb 124 g/L; sodium 132 mmol/L; creatinine 130 micromol/L; glucose 14.2 mmol/L; LRINEC score 9

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: MRI of the left calf to confirm fascial involvement before any surgical or antibiotic intervention.
options.rationale: Imaging must not delay theatre or antibiotics when clinical and biochemical features are highly suggestive of necrotising infection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

options.text: Urgent surgical debridement plus IV piperacillin-tazobactam, clindamycin, and vancomycin.
options.rationale: LRINEC at or above 8 has a high probability of necrotising fasciitis; surgical debridement plus broad-spectrum antibiotics including clindamycin for toxin suppression is first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

options.text: Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily plus rest and elevation as outpatient cellulitis treatment.
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics and outpatient management are inadequate for septic shock with calf pain out of proportion. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

options.text: Compartment pressure measurement to exclude acute compartment syndrome before surgical debridement.
options.rationale: Compartment syndrome is part of the differential but does not delay debridement when necrotising fasciitis is suspected. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

options.text: Hyperbaric oxygen as the primary therapy without immediate surgical exploration of the limb.
options.rationale: Hyperbaric oxygen is an adjunct; surgical debridement plus IV antibiotics remain the cornerstone of treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency. Clinical features include pain out of proportion to physical findings, tense oedema, dusky discolouration, haemorrhagic bullae, crepitus, anaesthesia of overlying skin, and rapidly progressive systemic toxicity. The LRINEC score (CRP, WCC, haemoglobin, sodium, creatinine, glucose) stratifies risk: at or above 6 is intermediate, at or above 8 is high. Treatment is urgent surgical debridement with IV piperacillin-tazobactam (or meropenem) plus vancomycin (for MRSA) plus clindamycin (for protein synthesis inhibition and toxin suppression). Imaging is supportive but should not delay theatre.
key_points: LRINEC at or above 8 is high probability of necrotising fasciitis.
Treatment: surgical debridement plus piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin plus clindamycin.
Pain out of proportion and anaesthesia of overlying skin are clinical red flags.
explanation.pearls: Clindamycin earns its place in the regimen by suppressing exotoxin production by Streptococcus pyogenes and Staphylococcus aureus, even if MIC favours another agent for bacterial killing.

stem:
A 34-year-old woman who recently arrived from Vietnam attends a refugee health clinic. She has had 3 weeks of dry cough, low-grade fevers in the evenings, drenching night sweats, and 4 kg of unintentional weight loss. Her QuantiFERON-Gold Plus test is positive. The clinic registrar wants to know whether to treat as latent or active tuberculosis. The chest radiograph shows right upper lobe airspace opacity with a small cavity, and three early-morning sputum samples have been sent for acid-fast smear, culture, and Mycobacterium tuberculosis PCR. HIV testing is negative. The first sputum smear is just back as positive for acid-fast bacilli.

She has no other significant medical history and lives in shared accommodation with one other adult.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 96/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 95% on room air, temperature 37.8°C
data_table.Examination: Thin; right upper zone fine crackles; no clubbing; no peripheral lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: QFT-Plus positive; CXR right upper lobe airspace opacity with cavity; sputum AFB smear positive; HIV negative; LFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Start isoniazid 300 mg daily for 9 months as latent tuberculosis treatment with monthly LFT monitoring.
options.rationale: Cavitating CXR and smear-positive sputum indicate active TB, not latent, and require four-drug therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

options.text: Defer all treatment until liquid culture confirms drug susceptibility before any therapy commences.
options.rationale: Empirical four-drug therapy should start immediately for smear-positive active TB; cultures and susceptibility refine the regimen later. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

options.text: Isolate, notify the public health unit, and start four-drug therapy with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol.
options.rationale: Active pulmonary TB with smear-positive disease mandates respiratory isolation, public health notification, and the standard RIPE regimen for 2 months induction. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

options.text: Start rifampicin plus isoniazid for 3 months as a short-course latent tuberculosis regimen now.
options.rationale: Rifampicin plus isoniazid for 3 months is a latent TB regimen and is inadequate for confirmed active disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

options.text: Start moxifloxacin monotherapy 400 mg daily while awaiting susceptibility testing on the sputum specimen.
options.rationale: Fluoroquinolone monotherapy is contraindicated; it risks rapid resistance and is reserved for multidrug-resistant TB regimens under specialist supervision. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Active pulmonary tuberculosis is diagnosed in any patient with consistent symptoms (cough at least 3 weeks, weight loss, night sweats), abnormal chest radiograph (especially upper lobe airspace disease with cavitation), and positive sputum acid-fast smear or culture. A positive QuantiFERON shows TB infection but cannot distinguish latent from active disease; the clinical and radiological picture does that. Standard induction is rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol (RIPE) for 2 months, then rifampicin and isoniazid for a further 4 months, with directly observed therapy ideally and public health notification mandatory.
key_points: Cavitating CXR plus smear-positive sputum equals active TB regardless of QFT.
RIPE for 2 months then RH for 4 months is standard sensitive-strain therapy.
Notify public health and isolate from the moment active TB is suspected.
explanation.pearls: Add pyridoxine 25 mg daily to any isoniazid-containing regimen to prevent peripheral neuropathy, particularly in malnourished, pregnant, or HIV-positive patients.

stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 28 hours of fever, dry cough, headache, and myalgia. Her temperature peaked at 38.8°C. She has COPD on tiotropium and salbutamol with one hospital admission in the last year for an exacerbation. She has not received the seasonal influenza vaccine this year and there are confirmed influenza A cases in her residential community. The nasal swab influenza A PCR is positive. Her oxygen saturation is 93% on room air and her chest is clear. She has had no recent overseas travel. CXR shows no consolidation.

The ED team is considering antiviral therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 96/min, blood pressure 132/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 93% on room air, temperature 38.4°C
data_table.Examination: Mild respiratory distress; chest scattered wheeze with no crackles; no oral thrush
data_table.Investigations: Influenza A PCR positive; CXR no consolidation; FBC WCC 9.2 x10^9/L; CRP 42 mg/L; creatinine 78 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Withhold oseltamivir because the 48 hour window from symptom onset has now closed for benefit.
options.rationale: The 48 hour rule applies to low-risk healthy outpatients; high-risk patients benefit even when treatment starts later. (Source: CDNA influenza guidelines)

options.text: Start zanamivir inhaled 10 mg twice daily for 5 days as first-line over oseltamivir for this patient.
options.rationale: Inhaled zanamivir is contraindicated in patients with underlying airway disease such as COPD because it can precipitate bronchospasm. (Source: CDNA influenza guidelines)

options.text: Start amantadine 100 mg twice daily for 5 days targeting influenza A neuraminidase replication.
options.rationale: Amantadine is no longer recommended for seasonal influenza because of widespread resistance and a poor side effect profile. (Source: CDNA influenza guidelines)

options.text: Start oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily for 5 days because she is high risk despite 28 hours of symptoms.
options.rationale: Oseltamivir is recommended for high-risk groups including COPD even when symptoms have lasted beyond 48 hours, because of mortality benefit. (Source: CDNA influenza guidelines)

options.text: Provide supportive care alone with paracetamol and oral fluids until symptoms resolve over coming days.
options.rationale: Supportive care alone misses the morbidity and mortality benefit of antiviral therapy in high-risk patients. (Source: CDNA influenza guidelines)

explanation.summary: Antiviral therapy for laboratory-confirmed influenza is recommended for high-risk patients (chronic lung disease, chronic cardiovascular disease, chronic kidney disease, diabetes, immunocompromise, pregnancy or postpartum, age 65 or above, severe obesity, Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander adults, residents of aged care) and for any patient with severe illness. Oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily for 5 days is first-line; treatment within 48 hours is most effective but later treatment still reduces complications in high-risk groups. Inhaled zanamivir is contraindicated in chronic airway disease.
key_points: High-risk groups benefit from oseltamivir even after 48 hours.
Oseltamivir 75 mg twice daily for 5 days is first-line.
Avoid inhaled zanamivir in COPD or asthma due to bronchospasm risk.
explanation.pearls: Reduce oseltamivir to 30 mg twice daily for an eGFR of 30 to 60 mL/min/1.73m2, and to 30 mg daily for an eGFR of 10 to 30 mL/min/1.73m2; standard dosing risks accumulation toxicity in renal impairment.

stem:
A 42-year-old woman with known Addison's disease maintained on hydrocortisone 20 mg morning and 10 mg afternoon, plus fludrocortisone 100 micrograms daily, presents to the emergency department with 24 hours of vomiting, abdominal pain, and lethargy. She has been unable to keep her oral hydrocortisone down. She has not taken her stress dose. She is now drowsy. Her bedside glucose is 3.2 mmol/L and a venous blood gas has just returned. The triage nurse has placed two large-bore IV cannulas and bloods are off to the lab.

The ED registrar is preparing the next interventions.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 128/min, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 37.6°C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable; dry mucous membranes; capillary refill 3 seconds; abdomen soft, mildly tender
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 128 mmol/L; potassium 5.4 mmol/L; glucose 3.2 mmol/L; creatinine 142 micromol/L; VBG pH 7.31, lactate 2.4 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Hydrocortisone 20 mg AM and 10 mg PM; fludrocortisone 100 micrograms mane

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Double her oral hydrocortisone dose to 40 mg and observe for 4 hours before discharging home with follow-up.
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis requires parenteral therapy because oral absorption is unreliable in vomiting and shock. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: endocrinology)

options.text: Give dexamethasone 4 mg IV stat alone and wait for cortisol level result before any IV fluid therapy.
options.rationale: Dexamethasone lacks mineralocorticoid activity and giving steroid alone without fluids fails to treat the hypovolaemia and hyponatraemia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: endocrinology)

options.text: Give 3 percent saline 100 mL to correct hyponatraemia before starting any glucocorticoid replacement therapy.
options.rationale: Hyponatraemia in adrenal crisis is corrected by cortisol replacement and 0.9 percent saline; hypertonic saline risks osmotic demyelination. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: endocrinology)

options.text: Commence fludrocortisone 200 micrograms orally now and hold IV steroids pending early morning cortisol.
options.rationale: Fludrocortisone has no role in the acute resuscitation of adrenal crisis; hydrocortisone IV provides both glucocorticoid and partial mineralocorticoid effect. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: endocrinology)

options.text: Give hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat, commence 0.9 percent saline at 1 L over the first hour, and treat hypoglycaemia.
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis is treated with immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg, rapid saline resuscitation, and glucose correction. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis is a medical emergency in any patient with known adrenal insufficiency who is unwell, vomiting, or shocked. Immediate management is IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat followed by 50 mg every 6 hours (or a continuous infusion of 200 mg per 24 hours), rapid 0.9% saline resuscitation 1 L over the first hour, glucose correction with 50 mL of 50% dextrose if hypoglycaemic, and treatment of any precipitant such as infection. High-dose hydrocortisone exerts sufficient mineralocorticoid effect that fludrocortisone is not needed during the acute phase. Sick-day rules: double oral hydrocortisone dose for intercurrent illness; switch to IM hydrocortisone for vomiting.
key_points: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV is the first parenteral dose in adrenal crisis.
Use 0.9 percent saline and glucose correction alongside steroid replacement.
Sick day rule: double oral steroid for intercurrent illness; IM hydrocortisone if vomiting.
explanation.pearls: Every Addison's patient should carry an emergency IM hydrocortisone kit (100 mg) and have a documented sick day plan; failure of community administration is a recurrent driver of preventable crisis admissions.

stem:
A 58-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with 18 hours of fever, severe headache, photophobia, and progressive confusion. He has hypertension and a remote left middle cerebral artery stroke with mild residual right-sided weakness. On arrival he opens his eyes to pain, says inappropriate words, and localises to pain (GCS 11). He has neck stiffness, no rash, and a new right-sided gaze preference. His pupils are 3 mm and reactive bilaterally with no papilloedema. Two large-bore IV cannulas are in. Blood cultures, FBC, U+E, coagulation, glucose, and lactate have been drawn.

The ED registrar is debating LP timing, antibiotic timing, and adjuvant steroid timing.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 102/min, blood pressure 168/92 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 39.1°C
data_table.Examination: GCS 11 (E2 V3 M5); meningism; new right gaze preference; pupils 3 mm reactive; no papilloedema; no rash
data_table.Investigations: Blood cultures drawn; WCC 18 x10^9/L; CRP 220 mg/L; INR 1.0; glucose 6.4 mmol/L; lactate 2.0 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next sequence of actions?

options.text: Give dexamethasone 10 mg IV, ceftriaxone 2 g IV, and benzylpenicillin 2.4 g IV, then arrange urgent CT brain before LP.
options.rationale: Focal neurology, altered consciousness, or seizure mandates CT before LP; dexamethasone and antibiotics must not wait for imaging, with benzylpenicillin added for Listeria cover above age 50. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

options.text: Perform immediate lumbar puncture, then give dexamethasone and ceftriaxone after the CSF specimen is in hand.
options.rationale: Focal neurological signs and reduced GCS contraindicate immediate LP because of coning risk; CT must precede LP here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

options.text: Give ceftriaxone 2 g IV and obtain CT brain first; defer dexamethasone until LP confirms bacterial pathogen.
options.rationale: Dexamethasone benefit in pneumococcal meningitis requires administration before or with the first antibiotic, not later. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

options.text: Give dexamethasone, ceftriaxone, vancomycin, and aciclovir empirically while waiting for CT and LP results.
options.rationale: Vancomycin is added when penicillin-resistant pneumococcus is suspected (overseas travel, prior antibiotics) and aciclovir if encephalitis is the leading diagnosis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

options.text: Mannitol 1 g/kg IV plus intubation to control intracranial pressure before any antibiotic therapy is delivered.
options.rationale: Antibiotic timing drives mortality in meningitis; intracranial pressure management must run alongside, not delay, antimicrobial therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Adult community-acquired bacterial meningitis is a time-critical emergency. Where altered consciousness, focal neurology, seizure, immunocompromise, or papilloedema is present, CT must precede LP to exclude a mass lesion; however, antibiotics and adjunctive dexamethasone must not wait. Adjuvant dexamethasone 10 mg IV every 6 hours for 4 days, given before or with the first antibiotic, reduces mortality and neurological sequelae from pneumococcal meningitis. Australian Therapeutic Guidelines empirical therapy is ceftriaxone 2 g IV plus benzylpenicillin 2.4 g IV every 4 hours when age above 50 or immunocompromise to cover Listeria, with vancomycin added if resistant pneumococcus is suspected.
key_points: Empirical adults: ceftriaxone 2 g IV; add benzylpenicillin if age above 50 or immunocompromised.
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV must be given before or with the first antibiotic dose.
CT before LP if focal neurology, GCS below 13, seizures, or papilloedema; antibiotics first.
explanation.pearls: Targeted dexamethasone benefit is greatest in pneumococcal meningitis and uncertain in meningococcal disease; once CSF gram stain shows meningococci, dexamethasone can be withdrawn.
